Western media employs double standards; when language becomes a form of reality. In the current international system, the media is not merely a conduit for news but a powerful mechanism for defining and constructing reality. What an average reader or viewer considers “truth” is often shaped through the media's selection, language, and framing. Consequently, the concepts of “double standards” and “media framing” are central to media studies. These concepts demonstrate how the media presents the same events with different political, ethical, and emotional colors. Numerous researchers in media studies believe that the language of the media is not neutral. In 2025, researchers at the University of Malaysia, in their contemporary paper “Media Framing and the Delegitimization of Palestinian Resistance,” confirmed this, noting how media outlets use language to distort perceptions. They specifically address how media framing influences perspectives on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. This discrepancy can be seen in how Western media covers the conflicts in Ukraine and Gaza, highlighting the stark differences in reporting and focus. The language, terminology, and emotional weight given to each situation differ significantly, showcasing a selective approach to portraying events and influencing public opinion. The language used to describe the casualties of the conflict between Israel and Hamas is carefully chosen. Israeli casualties are frequently named and presented with personal stories, fostering empathy and a sense of connection. Conversely, Palestinian casualties are often reduced to numbers, diminishing the sense of individual loss and humanity. This selective approach to humanization extends to broader narratives surrounding both conflicts. Ukraine receives extensive live coverage, in-depth analysis, and significant political response, while coverage of Gaza is often limited and focuses primarily on statistics. Similarly, the intensity of international sanctions and political pressure differs significantly, highlighting a pattern of “selective enforcement.” The roots of this double standard in Western media are deeply intertwined with historical and cultural contexts. As argued by Palestinian-American scholar Edward Said in his seminal work "Orientalism," the West portrays the East as “other,” often associating it with irrationality, violence, and backwardness. This kind of historical bias manifests in the language used by the media, leading to the portrayal of certain societies in a negative light. Ultimately, the language isn't merely a tool for communication but a subtle yet powerful instrument of influence, shaping perceptions and reinforcing existing power structures. Sabra has been arbitrarily detained in Yemen since 25 September 2025, when he was arrested at his office in Sana’a by Huthi armed forces and men in civilian clothing. They presented an arrest warrant and said his arrest was due to his social media posts marking the anniversary of Yemen’s 26th September Revolution. In December 2025, Sabra was held in solitary confinement for more than two weeks. He has gone on intermittent hunger strike to protest his treatment. Seven months after his arrest, he remains arbitrarily detained without formal charges. The Huthis must immediately release him and all those who remain in arbitrary detention. This includes dozens of UN and civil society staff that have been arrested, arbitrarily detained and forcibly disappeared during widespread crackdowns in 2024 and 2025.
